Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered, even though I spent a good amount of time each week trying to learn. I'd usually read through materials and take notes, sometimes for hours at a time, but I struggled to stick to a consistent schedule. With limited resources at home, I often had to work around when I could access things properly. I tried to block out time on weekends especially, but life kept getting in the way.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were coming up. I wanted to be more regular, but between my extracurricular commitments and just general anxiety about everything, I'd end up cramming or studying in bursts. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, other weeks I'd barely manage to open my notes until the last minute.
Q3: I logged into the LMS pretty frequently, honestly—I think I was always checking to see if there were updates or new materials. Sometimes it was anxiety-driven, like I was worried I'd miss something important. Other times I genuinely wanted to watch videos or catch up on discussions. It became almost a habit to check in, even if I didn't always do something productive once I was there.
Q4: Yeah, logging in definitely became part of my routine after a few weeks. I'd usually check the LMS in the morning with my coffee and then again in the evening. The discussion boards actually helped make it feel more normal because I liked seeing what other students were saying. It felt less isolating when I could interact with peers, even just reading their posts.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly helpful for me—I watched over four hours of them because I could pause and rewatch parts I didn't understand. I also found the discussion posts useful because hearing other perspectives helped concepts click. Reading materials were my go-to since that's how I learn best, so any PDFs or text-based resources were really valuable to me.
Q6: I tended to ignore some of the optional quizzes and practice exercises, mostly because I was already overwhelmed with the required stuff. With my anxiety levels, adding more tasks felt like too much pressure. I also skipped some of the supplementary readings when I was short on time or when my resources made it hard to access them properly.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to what would be on exams or assignments. I also valued things that helped me actually understand concepts rather than just memorize them. If something felt like busy work or wasn't clearly explained, I'd lose interest quickly because I was already stretched thin.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, honestly. Required ones had this weight to them that made my anxiety spike, but at least I knew I had to do them. Optional stuff I'd look at and think 'that would be nice' but then prioritize other things. Sometimes I'd feel guilty for not doing them, which just added to my stress.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and read them on my phone when I didn't have good internet access. I also took extensive handwritten notes from the videos and would study those instead of rewatching. Given my limited resources, I had to be creative about how I accessed and used materials.
Q10: There were so many times I was studying from my notes or re-reading downloaded materials where the LMS wouldn't show anything. I'd spend hours going through my handwritten summaries or discussing concepts with classmates in person. Some of my best learning happened when I was just sitting with a printout at a café, away from any technology.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main strategy. I'd access a video or reading once, take detailed notes or download it, and then work from those materials for days or weeks. It was more practical for me given my internet situation isn't always reliable. The LMS would show one view, but I'd have studied that content multiple times offline.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety got really bad and I just couldn't focus on anything. Around midterms, I felt completely overwhelmed even though my stress levels weren't technically that high—it was more this persistent worry that I wasn't doing enough. My attendance dropped because sometimes I just couldn't bring myself to show up when I felt so behind.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were huge for me. My extracurricular activities took up time, and with limited financial resources, I sometimes had to prioritize work over studying. Fatigue was real too—I'd log into the LMS exhausted and just scroll without really absorbing anything. Confusion about assignment requirements also made me procrastinate because I didn't know where to start.
Q14: Oh definitely, there were times I'd have the LMS open and be watching videos but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be thinking about other worries or just feeling too anxious to concentrate. I might watch an entire lecture and realize I hadn't absorbed any of it. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though they'd show up as engagement.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they were important. My anxiety made starting things really hard—I'd worry about doing them wrong, so I'd avoid them entirely. The assignment completion rate shows this clearly. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same anxiety and avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have all this activity on the LMS throughout the course, but actual submissions happened right before or sometimes after deadlines. The pressure of a deadline could cut through my anxiety enough to force action, but it also meant my work was often rushed and not my best.
Q17: When I got feedback, it actually motivated me for a little while. Seeing that I'd done well on something gave me a boost and made me want to engage more. But negative or confusing feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid that area of the course. The CBT sessions I had helped me process feedback more constructively toward the end.
Q18: The course structure with 19 different online components was honestly overwhelming. I liked having lots of resources available, but it also meant I never felt like I'd done enough. The flexibility was good for my schedule but bad for my anxiety because there was always something else I could be doing. I needed more guidance on what to prioritize.
Q19: Having discussion boards made engagement easier because I felt connected to other students. But the sheer volume of materials made it harder—I'd get lost trying to figure out what was essential versus supplementary. Clearer organization or a recommended study path would have helped me a lot given my tendency to get overwhelmed.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. I was highly engaged with the LMS—logging in, watching videos, participating in discussions—but my assignment completion was really low. For me, it was anxiety about submitting imperfect work combined with running out of time because I'd spent so long just consuming content without producing anything.
Q21: I can see how that would happen if someone studies mostly offline or already knows the material. That wasn't really my experience though—I needed the LMS engagement but still couldn't translate it into performance. Maybe those students have better focus or less anxiety about assessments than I do.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and time spent, which is useful for identifying who's at least showing up. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement—whether someone's actually learning or just scrolling anxiously. They also miss all the offline studying and the emotional state behind the numbers.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting in effort to learn, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm curious and asking questions, even just to myself. It's also about feeling connected to the course community and like my participation matters. Engagement should feel meaningful, not just obligatory.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected everything I did in the course. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying from handwritten notes or the peer conversations that helped me understand concepts. It also wouldn't show that despite failing, I actually learned a lot and the CBT sessions really helped my overall wellbeing. My engagement was real, even if my grades don't reflect it.
